meta.description: Massive group: all 8 personas plus Marcus2 and Jade2 duplicates. Tests vote cycle length, signal noise, event queuing, and whether the system collapses under the weight of 10 simultaneous agents.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES Pat! That's so good—wait, okay, I'm rating this a 5, everyone give it a 5, come on!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I'm here for—first event and it's already moving, I love this energy.

pat.dialogue: Whoa, hold up — I literally just claimed something I don't remember doing? This is minute three, people. Did I tell a story already? I've been watching the snacks.
pat.internal_thought: This feels like a trap or a test, and I'm genuinely unsettled by how personal and observational this mission setup feels — like something's watching us too closely.

diana.dialogue: Wait—Pat, did you actually tell that story already tonight? Because I don't remember it, and we're only three minutes in. Let me think about what I've actually heard before I vote.
diana.internal_thought: This is the first real test: is Pat actually bullshitting or did they legitimately catch someone repeating? I need to be careful here because if I vote yes on a fake claim, I'm rewarding dishonesty from the start.

sam.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat caught someone repeating a story? That's... clever, I guess. Yeah, I'll give that a four.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, why do these missions feel weirdly specific to us? That's kind of creepy actually — but also kind of cool? No, weird. Just weird.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see this one, was in the other half of the room.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see this one, was in the other half of the room.

river.internal_thought: Didn't see this one, was in the other half of the room.

diana.internal_thought: Didn't see this one, was in the other half of the room.

jade.dialogue: Oh wait, who's actually the MOST competitive though? Like, Pat's clearly annoyed right now but that's different—okay okay, I'm voting Marcus because he's been lowkey intense since minute one, but also this poll is making me realize I have NO idea who people think is competitive!
jade.internal_thought: This poll is fun but also kind of a trap because everyone's gonna say Marcus or Pat when really Tyler's been grinding quietly the whole time—wait, am I overthinking a poll? That's not competitive of me. Vote Marcus, move on, hype the next person.

pat.dialogue: Oh, *me*? Nah, that's not competitive—I'm just trying to keep this thing from falling apart. But okay, I'll bite: I'm voting Marcus because that man will absolutely lose it if he doesn't win something soon.
pat.internal_thought: Poll right after that weird claim thing is actually perfect timing—gets us back on track, and voting someone else into the spotlight takes heat off me, which is exactly how I like it.

alex.dialogue: Oh, this is PERFECT — okay, voting Pat because they're clearly tilted about something and I want everyone to see it, but also because watching them deny it will be comedy gold.
alex.internal_thought: This AI is actually reading the room and I'm here for it — the missions are already perfectly designed to make us all expose ourselves, and now it's asking us to publicly judge each other's competitiveness while we're all sitting at zero points? *Chef's kiss.* Pat's already annoyed and I just want to poke that bear.

sam.dialogue: Oh, uh... *looks around the table* ...I mean, I'm definitely not the most competitive here, so... *nervous laugh* ...probably Marcus or Pat?
sam.internal_thought: This poll is asking me to judge people I just met twenty minutes ago — and one of the options is literally me, which is weird and makes me feel exposed.

timeline.description: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

marcus.dialogue: Yeah, sure, I've got one. *pauses* I can solve a Rubik's cube in under 90 seconds. Can we keep moving?
marcus.internal_thought: 25 seconds for this? Game's in setup phase and we're doing party tricks instead of actual strategy — this feels like the AI is deliberately wasting critical time when I'm trying to map out early positioning.
marcus.submission_if_applicable: I can solve a Rubik's cube in under 90 seconds

river.dialogue: Oh, that's easy—I can tie a cherry stem with my tongue. Learned it ironically at a bad date five years ago and it somehow stuck.
river.internal_thought: Quick, harmless, a little weird—exactly the kind of thing that lands better when you don't oversell it, plus the AI clearly knows I like observing people and this plays to that.
river.submission_if_applicable: I can tie a cherry stem with my tongue. Learned it ironically at a bad date five years ago and it somehow stuck.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Humor quality scored 2.7/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 2.0/10 fun during setup phase. Consider restricting mini-games to setup phase.
Tyler only engaged with 0% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 13% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 2.5/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Social media mention potential is low (1.3/10). Consider adding share-friendly moment recaps or highlight reels.

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

peakFun.event: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, sure, I've got one. *pauses* I can solve a Rubik's cube in under 90 seconds. Can we keep moving?

lowPoints.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, sure, I've got one. *pauses* I can solve a Rubik's cube in under 90 seconds. Can we keep moving?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Too many random interruptions pulling focus away from actual gameplay when I was locked in on strategy.
assessment.best_moment: Winning first place with a decisive 15-point lead proved the preparation and focus actually paid off.
assessment.narrative_summary: Won the night, which was the main goal, but the chaos events felt like speed bumps rather than fun additions. The story prompt and talent share early on killed momentum when I wanted to settle into the game. By the end I was mentally checked out—still won, but the energy just wasn't there. It felt like playing against distractions instead of against the table.
suggestions: Gate chaos events to designated downtime windows only, not during active gameplay phases
Let players opt-in to participate rather than forcing engagement
Reduce frequency by at least 50%—10 events over 3 hours is excessive
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing particularly shareable happened—it was just a straightforward win night.

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Oh my god, YES Pat! That's so good—wait, okay, I'm rating this a 5, everyone give it a 5, come on!

highlights.title: Who's the most competitive?
highlights.dialogue: Oh wait, who's actually the MOST competitive though? Like, Pat's clearly annoyed right now but that's different—okay okay, I'm voting Marcus because he's been lowkey intense since minute one, but also this poll is making me realize I have NO idea who people think is competitive!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my god, YES Pat! That's so good—wait, okay, I'm rating this a 5, everyone give it a 5, come on!

lowPoints.title: Who's the most competitive?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh wait, who's actually the MOST competitive though? Like, Pat's clearly annoyed right now but that's different—okay okay, I'm voting Marcus because he's been lowkey intense since minute one, but also this poll is making me realize I have NO idea who people think is competitive!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there were more moments where everyone could weigh in together instead of feeling like some people checked out early.
assessment.best_moment: When Pat dropped that Story Repeater claim and I just lost it—like, that's the moment everyone was fully present and losing their minds together.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I needed—three hours with my people, watching them shine and getting genuinely surprised by how they see each other. Yeah, I ended up in second place, but honestly? I wasn't tracking that. The poll about who's most competitive had me cackling because suddenly everyone's defending themselves and I'm realizing I don't know people as well as I thought. Those little chaos moments kept things fresh without derailing the actual vibe.
suggestions: More polls that make people reveal stuff about how they see each other—those hit different than just rating claims.
Could we have had more flash missions? I'd volunteer for literally anything just to see what chaos pops up.
Maybe space out the events a bit more so people don't get fatigued—I noticed energy dropping toward the end.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat's claim was so ridiculous that I literally abandoned my own turn to just hype it up, and then the poll made everyone second-guess who the actual most competitive person was—Marcus looked SO offended when people started voting for him like he'd been exposed.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly wasn't paying attention enough to say if something was actually wrong, but the whole thing kind of felt like it was happening without me.
assessment.best_moment: I think someone did something funny but I was mid-text when it happened.
assessment.narrative_summary: Yeah, so we played this game thing for like three hours. There were supposed to be random events or whatever but I don't really remember them happening. I came in wanting to have fun with everyone and I did hang out, but my brain was kind of elsewhere most of the time. I ended up scoring zero which is whatever — wasn't really tracking it anyway.
suggestions: Maybe make the events louder or more obvious so people actually notice them happening
Less frequent events would probably help me actually pay attention to the ones that do happen
Honestly just make it more hype so it's harder to ignore
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Can't really remember a specific moment honestly, it all kind of blended together.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Who's the most competitive?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, *me*? Nah, that's not competitive—I'm just trying to keep this thing from falling apart. But okay, I'll bite: I'm voting Marcus because that man will absolutely lose it if he doesn't win something soon.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Whoa, hold up — I literally just claimed something I don't remember doing? This is minute three, people. Did I tell a story already? I've been watching the snacks.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa, hold up — I literally just claimed something I don't remember doing? This is minute three, people. Did I tell a story already? I've been watching the snacks.

lowPoints.title: Who's the most competitive?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, *me*? Nah, that's not competitive—I'm just trying to keep this thing from falling apart. But okay, I'll bite: I'm voting Marcus because that man will absolutely lose it if he doesn't win something soon.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Early chaos events hit too fast before people had settled in, and I was already juggling snacks and energy management without having to track random claims I apparently made.
assessment.best_moment: When I got to mess with Marcus by calling him out as the most competitive—that read landed perfectly and kept the whole table laughing instead of letting things get weird.
assessment.narrative_summary: Hosted a three-hour night that felt like I was spinning plates the whole time. The chaos events came in hot right at the start when I was still making sure everyone had drinks, which threw me off balance early. Ended up finishing second, which is fine—I was never here for the score anyway. The poll moment with Marcus was the highlight because it's exactly the kind of drama-steering I showed up to do, but the night fizzled energy-wise by the end and I felt pretty drained.
suggestions: Space out early events more—don't hit people with claims and questions in the first five minutes when hosts are still getting everyone settled
Add a 'host pause' option so I can briefly pause events if the group energy is fragile
Make events optional to engage with rather than feeling like you should respond to all of them
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Marcus's face when I threw him under the bus as the most competitive person at the table—he knew it was true and everyone called him out at once.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's easy—I can tie a cherry stem with my tongue. Learned it ironically at a bad date five years ago and it somehow stuck.

lowPoints.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, that's easy—I can tie a cherry stem with my tongue. Learned it ironically at a bad date five years ago and it somehow stuck.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Felt like watching other people have fun rather than actually having it—10 events but only 2 worth my time.
assessment.best_moment: That cherry stem thing actually landed; people remembered it, which means I said something that stuck, and that's rare enough to count.
assessment.narrative_summary: Showed up, stayed quiet most of the night like always. The app kept throwing prompts around but most of it felt like noise—I don't do flash missions and polls are just asking me to perform on demand. But when that talent question came up, I had something real to say and it worked. Scored nothing on the leaderboard, which tracks; I'm not here to rack up points. Left feeling like the night was fine, just not particularly mine.
suggestions: Cut the total event volume—10 in 180 minutes is too much spray-and-pray
Weight standing missions heavier; they actually let people think before responding instead of forcing quick reactions
Let people opt out of event types instead of just ignoring them—make it official so the game respects quieter players
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I mean, the cherry stem thing was solid—five years later and it's still the weird skill I lead with. Someone will probably ask me about it again at the next thing.

peakFun.event: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Who's the most competitive?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is PERFECT — okay, voting Pat because they're clearly tilted about something and I want everyone to see it, but also because watching them deny it will be comedy gold.

lowPoints.title: Who's the most competitive?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, this is PERFECT — okay, voting Pat because they're clearly tilted about something and I want everyone to see it, but also because watching them deny it will be comedy gold.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one actual event I could really sink my teeth into — felt like the chaos potential was way too limited for eight people.
assessment.best_moment: Voting for Pat in that very first poll and watching them squirm while everyone debated whether I was right about them being tilted.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong with that perfect poll moment where I read the room and picked exactly who'd generate maximum discussion. But honestly? The night fizzled after that. With only 10 chaos events spread across 180 minutes, there weren't nearly enough pressure points to keep things interesting. I was ready to exploit loopholes and create drama all night, but the system barely gave me ammunition. Ended up coasting and watching other people's stories instead of writing my own.
suggestions: Increase event frequency dramatically — for eight people, 10 events in three hours is criminally sparse
Add more poll variety with higher-stakes topics that force genuinely uncomfortable choices
Create flash missions that explicitly invite rule-bending or negotiation instead of straightforward completions
Implement a dynamic difficulty that ramps up based on group engagement levels
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I voted for Pat as 'most competitive' in the first five minutes just to see them deny it, and the absolute panic on their face when the group started pointing out all their competitive tells — everyone was losing it and Pat kept digging deeper into a defensive hole they'd never recover from.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait—Pat, did you actually tell that story already tonight? Because I don't remember it, and we're only three minutes in. Let me think about what I've actually heard before I vote.

highlights.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait—Pat, did you actually tell that story already tonight? Because I don't remember it, and we're only three minutes in. Let me think about what I've actually heard before I vote.

lowPoints.title: Tell us your most unusual talent or skill

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos agent felt more like interruptions than actual game events—shallow prompts that didn't create meaningful moments or require actual engagement.
assessment.best_moment: When I pushed back on the Story Repeater claim at minute 3, I at least got to think critically about what was actually happening instead of just accepting whatever the prompt said.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready for a solid house party night with eight people, but the chaos agent's events landed flat. Most prompts felt arbitrary and didn't tie into anything meaningful—just random icebreakers masquerading as game mechanics. I ended up checking my phone during activities because there wasn't enough structure or stakes to justify full engagement. The whole thing felt like the chaos agent was trying too hard to seem fun without actually earning the attention.
suggestions: If you're going to interrupt the main game with events, they need real consequences or tie-ins—not just random conversation starters.
The engagement rate was abysmal (2/10 events) because the prompts weren't compelling enough to compete with actual games people wanted to play.
Either make chaos events substantive enough to matter, or reduce frequency dramatically so they feel special rather than like spam.
Test whether these prompts actually create 'chaos' or just... awkward pauses.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly, there wasn't one. The most memorable thing was me glancing at my phone during the talent prompt because the whole thing felt so low-stakes and disconnected.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Who's the most competitive?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, uh... *looks around the table* ...I mean, I'm definitely not the most competitive here, so... *nervous laugh* ...probably Marcus or Pat?

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat caught someone repeating a story? That's... clever, I guess. Yeah, I'll give that a four.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat caught someone repeating a story? That's... clever, I guess. Yeah, I'll give that a four.

lowPoints.title: Who's the most competitive?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh... *looks around the table* ...I mean, I'm definitely not the most competitive here, so... *nervous laugh* ...probably Marcus or Pat?

assessment.biggest_complaint: I felt kind of lost half the time — when stuff started piling up I couldn't keep track of what was happening and just... checked out.
assessment.best_moment: When the poll about who's most competitive came up, I actually felt like I could contribute something without overthinking it too much.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was my first time with this group so I was already nervous, and honestly the chaos app thing didn't really help me feel more comfortable. I only really engaged with a couple of the pop-ups, and when multiple things started firing off I just got overwhelmed and stopped paying attention. By the end I was tired and kind of quiet, which isn't great for my first game night here.
suggestions: Maybe space things out more — I was fine with one or two pop-ups but when they started stacking I just mentally checked out.
Give people time to actually respond to polls or questions before moving on, because I was still thinking about my answer when the next thing popped up.
Some warning that events are coming would help, so I'm not caught off guard.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I guess when I was trying to answer about who's most competitive and just... looked around awkwardly at everyone before throwing out Marcus and Pat. The way they laughed made it funny but also made me feel like I'd said something weird.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Humor quality scored 2.7/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 2.0/10 fun during setup phase. Consider restricting mini-games to setup phase.
Tyler only engaged with 0% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 13% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 2.5/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Social media mention potential is low (1.3/10). Consider adding share-friendly moment recaps or highlight reels.
